title: Chapter 3: TensorFlow - Quiz
description: Test your understanding of TensorFlow, tensors, Keras API, and building neural network models

questions.question: What is a tensor in TensorFlow?

options.text: A type of neural network layer
A multi-dimensional array of data that serves as the fundamental data structure in TensorFlow
A function that calculates gradients automatically
A configuration file for defining model architecture

questions.explanation: A tensor is a multi-dimensional array — the core data structure in TensorFlow. A scalar is a rank-0 tensor, a vector is rank-1, a matrix is rank-2, and higher-dimensional arrays are rank-3+. Tensors have a shape (dimensions), a dtype (data type), and are immutable. All data in TensorFlow flows through the computation graph as tensors.

questions.question: What is the Keras API in TensorFlow?

options.text: A low-level C++ interface for defining custom GPU operations
A high-level API integrated into TensorFlow that provides user-friendly abstractions for building, training, and evaluating neural network models
A separate library that cannot be used with TensorFlow
A visualization tool for monitoring training progress

questions.explanation: Keras is a high-level neural network API that is fully integrated into TensorFlow as tf.keras. It provides intuitive abstractions like Sequential and Functional model APIs, pre-built layers, optimizers, and loss functions, making it easy to build and train deep learning models without writing low-level TensorFlow code.

questions.question: What is the difference between the Sequential API and the Functional API in Keras?

options.text: Sequential is for GPU and Functional is for CPU only
Sequential stacks layers linearly one after another, while the Functional API allows building complex architectures with multiple inputs, outputs, and shared layers
Sequential is newer and more powerful than the Functional API
There is no difference; they produce identical model architectures

questions.explanation: The Sequential API creates models by stacking layers in a linear sequence — each layer has exactly one input and one output. The Functional API is more flexible, supporting models with multiple inputs/outputs, shared layers, residual connections, and arbitrary graph topologies. Use Sequential for simple architectures and Functional for complex ones.

questions.question: What does model.compile() do in Keras?

options.text: It converts the Python code to C++ for faster execution
It configures the model for training by specifying the optimizer, loss function, and evaluation metrics
It trains the model on the provided data
It saves the model to disk in a compiled binary format

questions.explanation: model.compile() configures the learning process before training. It requires specifying: an optimizer (e.g., 'adam', 'sgd') that determines how weights are updated, a loss function (e.g., 'binary_crossentropy', 'mse') that measures prediction error, and optionally metrics (e.g., 'accuracy') for monitoring during training.

questions.question: Which loss function should you use for a multi-class classification problem where labels are one-hot encoded?

options.text: binary_crossentropy
mean_squared_error
categorical_crossentropy
hinge

questions.explanation: categorical_crossentropy is used for multi-class classification when labels are one-hot encoded (e.g., [0, 1, 0] for class 1 of 3). If labels are integers instead, use sparse_categorical_crossentropy. binary_crossentropy is for two-class problems. mean_squared_error is typically used for regression tasks.

questions.question: What does the Adam optimizer combine?

options.text: Gradient descent and random search
The benefits of AdaGrad (adaptive learning rates) and RMSProp (moving average of squared gradients), with momentum
Supervised and unsupervised learning techniques
Batch and online learning approaches

questions.explanation: Adam (Adaptive Moment Estimation) combines ideas from AdaGrad (which adapts the learning rate per parameter based on historical gradients) and RMSProp (which uses a moving average of squared gradients). Adam also maintains moving averages of both the gradients (first moment) and squared gradients (second moment), with bias correction. It is widely used because it works well with default hyperparameters.

questions.question: What does model.fit() do in Keras?

options.text: It adjusts the model architecture to fit the input shape
It trains the model on the provided training data for a specified number of epochs and batch size
It evaluates the model on test data and returns accuracy
It loads pre-trained weights from a file

questions.explanation: model.fit() trains the model by iterating over the training data for the specified number of epochs, updating weights through forward passes, loss computation, and backpropagation. Key parameters include epochs (number of full passes), batch_size (samples per gradient update), and validation_data (for monitoring overfitting during training).

questions.question: What is the purpose of the Dense layer in Keras?

options.text: It compresses the input data to reduce memory usage
It is a fully connected layer where every neuron is connected to every neuron in the previous layer
It applies convolutional filters to the input
It randomly drops neurons during training to prevent overfitting

questions.explanation: A Dense layer (fully connected layer) connects every input neuron to every output neuron, each connection having a learnable weight. It computes output = activation(dot(input, kernel) + bias). Dense layers are the most common layer type in neural networks and are used in most architectures. The first argument specifies the number of output neurons.

questions.question: How do you evaluate a trained Keras model on test data?

options.text: model.test(x_test, y_test)
model.evaluate(x_test, y_test)
model.score(x_test, y_test)
model.validate(x_test, y_test)

questions.explanation: model.evaluate() computes the loss and any metrics specified during model.compile() on the provided test data. It returns a list of scalars: the test loss and any additional metrics (e.g., accuracy). For getting predictions without evaluation, use model.predict(x_test), which returns the model's output values.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the softmax activation function in the output layer?

options.text: To ensure all output values are negative
To convert the output into a probability distribution where all values sum to 1, with each value between 0 and 1
To select only the highest output value and set all others to zero
To apply dropout regularization at inference time

questions.explanation: The softmax function converts a vector of raw scores (logits) into a probability distribution: each output value is between 0 and 1, and all outputs sum to 1. It is used in the output layer for multi-class classification, where each output represents the probability that the input belongs to a particular class. The class with the highest probability is the prediction.
